title: Death rate by cause
subtitle: The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and [non-communicable diseases](#dod:non-communicable-diseases).
caption:
Death rate by cause, World

The estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people from broad categories of causes of death. Injuries (such as accidents, violence and suicides); communicable, maternal, neonatal and nutritional diseases; and non-communicable diseases.

This stacked area chart tracks the estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people globally from 1980 to 2023, divided into three broad cause categories: non-communicable diseases (NCDs), communicable diseases (including maternal, neonatal, and nutritional causes), and injuries. The vertical axis spans from 0 to 1,400 deaths per 100,000, with each colored band representing one cause group’s contribution to the total. In 1980, the combined death rate stood near 1,300 per 100,000, with NCDs forming the largest base layer at roughly 800, communicable diseases adding about 400 above that, and injuries contributing a thin top layer of approximately 100. Over the next four decades, the total rate declined steadily, falling below 800 by 2010 and reaching around 700 by 2023. The green band for communicable diseases shows the steepest drop, shrinking from nearly 400 in 1980 to under 200 by 2023. The pink NCD layer also contracts, though less sharply, moving from 800 to just over 500. The tan injuries band remains relatively flat throughout, hovering between 80 and 120, with a small visible spike around 2020 followed by a dip and partial recovery by 2023. The overall shape of the stacked areas slopes downward consistently until 2019, then exhibits a brief upward blip before resuming its decline. The dominant pattern across the full period is a sustained reduction in mortality from all three causes, with the largest absolute decreases occurring in communicable diseases and non-communicable diseases, while injury-related deaths remain comparatively stable.
